description: Suricata rule structure definitions from official documentation — actions, protocols, addressing, ports, direction, and rule options

rule_structure.description: A Suricata rule consists of three parts: action, header, and rule options
rule_structure.example: alert http $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET any (msg:"HTTP GET Request Containing Rule in URI"; flow:established,to_server; http.method; content:"GET"; http.uri; content:"rule"; fast_pattern; classtype:bad-unknown; sid:123; rev:1;)

actions.description: The action determines what happens when the rule matches. AWS Network Firewall supports four actions.

values.name: pass
values.description: Stop further inspection of the packet

values.name: drop
values.description: Drop packet and generate alert (IPS mode)

values.name: reject
values.description: Send RST/ICMP unreachable error to sender of matching packet

actions.note: Standard Suricata also supports rejectsrc, rejectdst, and rejectboth, but AWS Network Firewall only supports the four actions listed above.

protocols.description: The protocol limits which protocol(s) the signature will be applied to. If protocol is 'http', Suricata ensures the signature only matches if the TCP stream contains HTTP traffic.

tcp_specific.name: tcp-pkt
tcp_specific.description: Match content in individual TCP packets

tcp_specific.name: tcp-stream
tcp_specific.description: Match content only in reassembled TCP stream

addressing.description: Source and destination IP addresses. Supports IPv4, IPv6, CIDR ranges, variables, grouping, and negation.

variables.name: $HOME_NET
variables.description: Internal network as defined in suricata.yaml

variables.name: $EXTERNAL_NET
variables.description: External network as defined in suricata.yaml

ports.description: Source and destination ports. Source ports are typically 'any' (OS assigns random). Destination ports identify the application.

direction.description: The directional arrow indicates which way the signature is evaluated.

values.description: Unidirectional — only packets from source to destination match
Bidirectional — rule matches in either direction (Suricata duplicates the rule internally)

notes: For AWS Network Firewall, use -> for most rules and <> for bidirectional inspection

rule_options.description: Options are enclosed in parentheses, separated by semicolons. Some have settings (keyword:value;), others are standalone (keyword;). Order matters — changing order changes rule meaning.
rule_options.escaping: Characters ; and " must be escaped with backslash when used in option values. Backslash itself must also be escaped.

modifier_types.description: Older style — looks back at previous content match. Example: content:"index.php"; http_uri;
modifier_types.note: The modifier applies to the content keyword that precedes it

modifier_types.description: Newer style — buffer name comes first, all following keywords apply to that buffer. Example: http.uri; content:"/api/";
modifier_types.note: Preferred style. The buffer keyword must appear BEFORE the content match it modifies

rule_options.normalized_buffers: HTTP keywords, reassembled streams, TLS/SSL/SSH/FTP/DCERPC buffers are normalized — anomalous content is removed and packets are combined. The data is an interpretation, not raw bytes.

best_practices: Use sticky buffer syntax (http.uri; content:...) over legacy content modifier syntax (content:...; http_uri;)
Sticky buffers must appear BEFORE the content match they modify
Rule option order matters — changing order changes rule meaning
Use flow:established,to_server for client-to-server traffic on established connections
Use $HOME_NET and $EXTERNAL_NET variables instead of hardcoded IPs
Source ports are typically 'any' since OS assigns random ports
Use fast_pattern on the most unique content match to optimize performance
Use noalert with flowbits/xbits when setting state without generating alerts
Escape semicolons and quotes in option values with backslash
For HTTP body size checks, use http.content_len with byte_test (not dsize, which is per-packet only)
For AWS Network Firewall, use strict rule ordering with 'drop established' default action
CANNOT combine aws_url_category or aws_domain_category with geoip in the same rule — use separate rules
aws_url_category requires TLS inspection for HTTPS traffic; without it, use aws_domain_category instead
For HTTP/2 domain filtering with TLS inspection, use http2 protocol with http.request_header or http2.header_name
Use flow:stateless in xbits pass rules to match packets regardless of connection state
IP set references use @ prefix (e.g., @BETA) to reference VPC prefix list ARNs
The pcre keyword MUST be paired with content, tls.sni, http.host, http.uri, or dns.query — it cannot be used alone in AWS Network Firewall
Do NOT use the priority keyword in strict order rule groups — it is not supported and will cause errors
Thresholding keywords (threshold, detection_filter) have limited support in AWS Network Firewall — test thoroughly before relying on them

aws_specific_keywords.description: Keywords specific to AWS Network Firewall that are not part of standard Suricata

keywords.name: aws_url_category
keywords.description: Match AWS managed URL categories. Evaluates complete URLs (HTTP) or domains. Requires TLS inspection for HTTPS. Supported protocol: HTTP. Example: aws_url_category:Malicious;

keywords.name: aws_domain_category
keywords.description: Match AWS managed domain categories. Evaluates domain from TLS SNI or HTTP host. No TLS inspection required. Supported protocols: TLS, HTTP. Example: aws_domain_category:Malware;

keywords.name: ja4.hash
keywords.description: JA4 TLS fingerprint hash sticky buffer. Newer than JA3, provides more granular TLS client fingerprinting.

keywords.name: dotprefix
keywords.description: Transform that prepends '.' to buffer content. Used with tls.sni or dns.query for subdomain matching. Example: tls.sni; dotprefix; content:".amazon.com"; endswith;

keywords.name: http2.header_name
keywords.description: HTTP/2 header name sticky buffer. Used for matching HTTP/2 pseudo-headers like 'authority'. Requires TLS inspection.

keywords.name: http.request_header
keywords.description: HTTP request header sticky buffer. Works with both HTTP/1 and HTTP/2. Format: content:"header_name|3a 20|value";

constraints: aws_url_category and aws_domain_category CANNOT be combined with geoip in the same rule
aws_url_category requires TLS inspection for HTTPS traffic evaluation
Multiple categories can be comma-separated: aws_url_category:Gambling,Social Networking;
Category database is automatically maintained and updated by AWS
The pcre keyword is only allowed with content, tls.sni, http.host, http.uri, and dns.query keywords — pcre cannot be used alone
The priority keyword is NOT supported for rule groups using strict evaluation order — do not include priority in strict order rules
Thresholding keywords (threshold, detection_filter) have limited support — test thoroughly before relying on them in production
